Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have much of a study routine. I'd study when I felt like it, which wasn't super often. Sometimes I'd spend a whole afternoon watching videos or looking at diagrams when something caught my interest, but then I'd go days without touching anything course-related. I know I put in around 25 hours a week total, but it was scattered and random, not planned at all.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place. I definitely wasn't one of those students who studies every Tuesday at 2pm or whatever. Sometimes I'd cram a bunch of hours in when I remembered something was due, other times I'd just hang out with friends from my extracurriculars instead. Deadlines sometimes pushed me to do something, but even then I often didn't follow through completely.
Q3: Usually I logged in because I saw other people in my peer group talking about something, or I got a notification that made me curious. Sometimes I'd log in just to scroll through and see what was happening, kind of like checking social media. It wasn't really about studying most of the time—more like seeing if I was missing anything interesting or if people were chatting about stuff.
Q4: Not really, if I'm being honest. I logged in pretty regularly—maybe five times a week—but it never felt like a routine. It was more of a casual habit, like I'd check it when I was bored or between other things. I never set a specific time to use it or made it part of my actual study schedule.
Q5: I liked the visual stuff the most—diagrams, infographics, videos with good graphics. I'm a visual learner so when something had clear images or animations, I actually paid attention and remembered it better. The discussion boards were also kind of fun because I could see what other people were thinking without having to do too much work myself.
Q6: I pretty much ignored most of the reading materials and the longer assignments. They just felt overwhelming and boring, and I didn't have the motivation to push through them. If something looked like a wall of text, I'd close it immediately. I kept telling myself I'd get to them later, but later never really came.
Q7: If it was quick, visual, and felt relevant to something I actually cared about, I'd do it. Activities that felt like busywork or took forever to complete just weren't worth it to me. I needed to see the point immediately or I'd lose interest. Honestly, if my friends were doing something or talking about it, that made it feel more worthwhile too.
Q8: Yeah, optional stuff felt way more chill and sometimes more interesting because there was no pressure. Required activities stressed me out even though I have low stress generally—it's more like I just avoided them because they felt like obligations. Optional things I could explore at my own pace, which suited me better even if I didn't always finish them either.
Q9: Definitely. I'd screenshot diagrams or download images and then study from my phone later when I wasn't near my laptop. I also had a notebook where I'd sketch out concepts visually because that helped me remember things. A lot of my actual learning happened while I was doodling or looking at saved pictures, not while I was logged into the LMS.
Q10: There were times I'd spend an hour redrawing a diagram from memory or explaining a concept to a friend from my club, and none of that showed up anywhere online. I'd also think about course stuff while doing other activities, like making connections in my head. The LMS only saw me for like 16 minutes of video, but I was processing things in my own way outside of it.
Q11: Oh yeah, all the time. I'd open a video or image once, screenshot or download it, and then never go back to the LMS for that material. I preferred having things saved on my phone where I could look at them whenever without needing internet or logging in. It was just more convenient for how I like to study.
Q12: There were definitely weeks where I just couldn't bring myself to care. Nothing bad was happening exactly—I was actually pretty happy overall—but the motivation just wasn't there. The course material felt disconnected from anything I was interested in, and my extracurriculars were way more engaging. It was easier to drift away from academics.
Q13: My extracurriculars took up a lot of my mental energy, and sometimes I was just tired from doing stuff I actually enjoyed. When I sat down to use the LMS, I'd feel this weird fatigue even though I wasn't stressed. Confusion about what I was supposed to prioritize also made me just... not do anything. It was easier to hang out with people than figure out the coursework.
Q14: Yeah, plenty of times. I'd scroll through the LMS, maybe click on a few things, but my brain was somewhere else entirely. I'd be thinking about plans with friends or something from my club activities. I was technically accessing materials but not absorbing anything. It was like my eyes were on the screen but my mind had checked out.
Q15: All the time. I knew assignments were important but I just kept pushing them off. Part of it was that they felt too big or confusing to start, and part of it was that I just didn't feel motivated enough to care. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow, and then tomorrow I'd say the same thing. My assignment completion rate shows that pretty clearly.
Q16: Deadlines would make me panic a little and log in more, but even then I often didn't actually submit things. I'd look at what was due, feel overwhelmed, maybe do a tiny bit, and then give up. The deadline pressure wasn't enough to overcome my lack of motivation. Sometimes I'd miss deadlines entirely and just accept it.
Q17: I didn't get much feedback because I didn't submit much work. When I did get feedback, it was kind of discouraging because it reminded me how behind I was. It didn't really motivate me to do more—if anything, it made me want to avoid the course even more. I know that's not a great reaction but that's honestly how it felt.
Q18: The structure felt pretty rigid and didn't match how I like to learn. Everything was laid out in a specific order with specific deadlines, but I work better when I can explore things that interest me. The peer interaction parts were nice though—I actually engaged with those because they felt more social and less like traditional schoolwork.
Q19: Having more visual content and interactive elements would have helped me a lot. The parts that were mostly text-based or required long reading sessions were really hard for me to engage with. Also, the discussion forums were good because they let me participate without doing heavy lifting. More flexibility in how to demonstrate learning would have been nice.
Q20: I think that's kind of me, actually. I logged in a lot and interacted with people, but my actual submissions were terrible. For me, it was about being present socially without committing to the hard work. Maybe some students are exploring, trying to figure things out, or just procrastinating like I was. Activity doesn't equal productivity.
Q21: That makes sense to me too. Some people are just efficient—they get what they need quickly and study offline. Or they're naturally good at the material and don't need to spend hours on the LMS. I did okay on my exam even though my LMS activity was kind of shallow, probably because I picked up enough from the visual stuff and peer conversations.
Q22: Logs can show when you clicked on things and how long you were technically on a page, but they can't show if you were actually paying attention or learning. They miss all the offline studying, the conversations with classmates, the thinking you do when you're not online. They also can't capture motivation or why someone isn't engaging.
Q23: To me, being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and wanting to understand it. It's not just about logging in or completing tasks—it's about being mentally present and curious. I wasn't really engaged this semester even though I was technically around. I showed up socially but checked out academically.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show that I was actually pretty happy this semester despite not doing well academically. It wouldn't show my rich social life through extracurriculars or how I learned things through conversations with peers outside the platform. It also wouldn't capture that I struggle with motivation even when I'm not stressed or anxious—I just drift.
